mechanism_narrative: The federal funds rate at 0.14% represents a zero lower bound environment where monetary policy is maximally accommodative. This compression of discount rates unambiguously supports equity valuations, producing positive S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days. The signal is always active and its predictive power decreases if the rate rises above 1%.
failure_condition: If the Rank IC of FEDFUNDS level vs forward 20-day returns is non-negative over any 2-year rolling window, or if forward returns are negative in >60% of periods when FEDFUNDS ≤ 0.5%.

mechanism_narrative: Unemployment rates sustained above 6% indicate a deeply weakened labor market with declining consumer spending and corporate earnings. This cyclical weakness translates into negative S&P 500 forward returns over the 20-day horizon. The signal activates when UNRATE exceeds 6.0%.
failure_condition: If the average forward 20-day return is non-negative in periods when UNRATE > 6.0% (sample at decision time), or if a t-test shows no significant difference from zero.

mechanism_narrative: When the CPI rises at a monthly rate above 0.2% while the fed funds rate remains below 0.5%, real interest rates are deeply negative. This erodes expected equity returns as inflation outpaces nominal yields, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over the 60-day horizon. The interaction of rising inflation and accommodative monetary policy is required for the signal.
failure_condition: If forward 60-day returns are positive in the majority of months where both CPIAUCSL month-over-month > 0.2% and FEDFUNDS < 0.5%, or if the interaction IC is below 0.05 over a 3-year window.

mechanism_narrative: In the current high-unemployment regime (UNRATE > 6%), increases in industrial production signal genuine output growth without supply constraints. This real improvement drives corporate earnings and positive S&P 500 forward returns over 60 days. The signal is regime-conditional and would reverse if unemployment dropped below 5%.
failure_condition: If the correlation between INDPRO monthly change and forward 60-day returns is negative or insignificant (p>0.05) during periods when UNRATE > 6%.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts falling below 900 thousand units signal a contraction in residential investment, a leading indicator of economic slowdown. This predicts negative S&P 500 forward returns over 20 trading days. The signal is triggered by HOUST data below the threshold.
failure_condition: If the average 20-day forward return is positive or not significantly different from zero during periods when HOUST < 900.

mechanism_narrative: Rising nonfarm payrolls consistently signal labor market strength and consumer spending growth, supporting corporate profits and positive S&P 500 forward returns over the 5-day horizon. This always-on momentum effect is a robust short-term predictor.
failure_condition: If the rolling 3-year Rank IC of PAYEMS monthly change vs forward 5-day returns is consistently below 0.0.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous month-over-month growth in retail sales and nonfarm payrolls confirms synchronized consumer and labor market strength. This positive demand-labor feedback loop generates upward earnings revisions and positive S&P 500 forward returns over 20 days. Both series must show growth for the signal to activate.
failure_condition: If the average 20-day forward return is not significantly positive (t-test p>0.10) during months when both RSAFS and PAYEMS show positive month-over-month growth.

mechanism_narrative: A month-over-month decline in the unemployment rate exceeding 0.3 percentage points represents a substantial labor market improvement, especially from elevated levels. This sharp tightening signals rising aggregate demand and forecasts positive S&P 500 forward returns over the 5-day horizon.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE declines by > 0.3 percentage points month-over-month
failure_condition: If the average 5-day forward return is negative or statistically insignificant (p>0.05) following months with UNRATE declines >0.3pp.
